title: Pneumonia in lambs
subtitle: Recognising the signs and acting before it costs you the lamb.
excerpt: Pneumonia is the leading cause of death in lambs from two weeks to weaning. It sets in fast, and a lamb that was feeding normally at the morning check can be found down and unresponsive by afternoon. Knowing what to look for, and having the right antibiotic available, often makes the difference between a lamb saved and one lost.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, Lamb Survival and Health Guide. DEFRA/APHA Sheep Welfare Code. SAC Consulting Scotland, Respiratory Disease in Lambs factsheet.

glossaryTerms.definition: The number of breaths per minute; a healthy lamb breathes 20 to 30 times per minute at rest. Over 40 is a sign of respiratory disease.
The thick, yellowish first milk produced by a ewe in the hours after lambing; rich in antibodies and energy, essential for lamb survival in the first 24 hours.

content.text: Pneumonia in lambs has a two-stage pattern. A respiratory virus (Parainfluenza 3 is the most common) weakens the airway lining. Bacteria move in behind it. Cold, damp, poorly ventilated housing makes both worse. Vaccination of the ewe passes some protection to the lamb via
, but is not always complete protection.
. Over 40 breaths per minute at rest is abnormal.
Nasal discharge, watery at first, thickening as the infection takes hold
Fever, a lamb's normal temperature is 38.5,40°C. Above 40°C is pyrexic; above 41°C needs antibiotic treatment the same day
Reluctance to move, standing apart from the group, not suckling
A grunting sound with each breath in severe cases, indicates pain on breathing

A lamb with suspected pneumonia and a temperature over 40°C needs antibiotics. Talk to your vet before lambing and have a product agreed and on the shelf. Waiting for a vet visit after the lamb has collapsed costs critical time. A prescription fever-reducer given alongside the antibiotic brings the temperature down quickly and improves recovery rates.

Move the sick lamb and its dam into a small individual pen. Keep the pen dry, draught-free, and bedded with fresh straw. If the lamb is refusing to feed, tube-feed colostrum or lamb milk replacer at 50 ml per kg bodyweight every four hours until it suckles again. Don't release back into the main flock until the lamb has been normal for 48 hours, recovery lambs are vulnerable to setbacks.
Vaccinate ewes six weeks and two weeks before lambing with a clostridial-plus-pasteurella product. The antibodies pass to the lamb in colostrum and protect it for the first eight weeks of life.
Ventilate the lambing shed at roof height. Cold fresh air is far better for lambs than warm, humid, still air.
Keep stocking density low. Pneumonia spreads rapidly through densely-packed lambs in a shared airspace.
